learning_objectives: Define health data management and its role in healthcare operations
Identify the lifecycle of health data from creation to archival
Understand the role of Health Information Management (HIM) professionals
readings: Health Information Management: Concepts, Principles, and Practice Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Health data management encompasses the processes and systems used to acquire, validate, store, protect, and use health data. As healthcare generates ever more data, effective management is critical.
key_concepts: Health data lifecycle: creation, storage, use, sharing, archival, destruction
HIM profession: evolution from medical records to data governance
Data quality dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity
Master Patient Index (MPI) and patient identity management
Legal health record vs. designated record set
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a hospital HIM department workflow from document receipt to coding to release of information

review.value: Database linking a patient's records across all systems using a unique identifier, critical for data integrity
The subset of patient data an organization defines as its official business record for legal and disclosure purposes

learning_objectives: Explain the principles of data governance in healthcare
Describe roles and responsibilities in a data governance program
Understand data stewardship at the organizational level

lecture_notes.introduction: Data governance provides the framework for ensuring health data is managed as a strategic asset. Without governance, data quality degrades and trust erodes.
key_concepts: Data governance defined: policies, processes, standards, accountability
Governance structure: data governance council, data stewards, data owners
Data dictionaries and metadata management
Data quality monitoring and remediation programs
Regulatory drivers: HIPAA, Cures Act, CMS conditions of participation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review of a sample data governance charter and data dictionary from a health system

review.value: Individual responsible for data quality within a specific domain, ensuring accuracy and proper use of data elements
Catalog defining each data element including name, type, valid values, source system, and business rules

learning_objectives: Apply ICD-10-CM coding principles to clinical scenarios
Understand the revenue cycle impact of coding accuracy
Describe computer-assisted coding and NLP-based coding tools
readings: CMS ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical coding translates clinical documentation into standardized codes that drive reimbursement, quality reporting, and research. Coding accuracy directly impacts organizational revenue and data integrity.
key_concepts: ICD-10-CM coding conventions and guidelines
Code structure: category, etiology, anatomic site, severity, laterality
Sequencing rules: principal diagnosis, secondary diagnoses, POA indicators
DRG assignment and its financial impact
Computer-Assisted Coding (CAC) and NLP-driven coding suggestions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on coding exercise: assign ICD-10-CM codes to three clinical scenarios of increasing complexity

review.value: The condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for the admission - determines DRG and reimbursement
Software that uses NLP to analyze clinical documentation and suggest appropriate diagnosis and procedure codes for coder review

learning_objectives: Describe health information exchange models and architectures
Understand the role of FHIR in modern health data exchange
Evaluate challenges in achieving true interoperability
readings: ONC Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA)

lecture_notes.introduction: Exchanging health data across organizational boundaries remains one of the greatest challenges in health informatics. Understanding exchange models and standards is essential.
key_concepts: HIE models: centralized, federated, hybrid
Query-based exchange vs. directed exchange vs. consumer-mediated
TEFCA: national framework for universal health data exchange
FHIR APIs for patient data access and population health
Barriers to interoperability: technical, organizational, political
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a FHIR API call to retrieve a patient's medication list from a test server
setup_requirements: FHIR test server access (HAPI FHIR or similar)

review.value: Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement - ONC's national approach to connecting health information networks for universal exchange
Model where data stays at the source organization and is queried on demand, maintaining local control while enabling exchange

class_schedule.topic: Release of Information and Privacy Management
learning_objectives: Apply HIPAA disclosure rules to release of information scenarios
Understand patient authorization requirements and exceptions
Describe the ROI process and its management in modern HIM departments

lecture_notes.introduction: Release of information (ROI) is where data governance meets patient rights. HIM professionals must balance timely access with privacy protection under complex regulations.
key_concepts: HIPAA permitted disclosures: treatment, payment, healthcare operations
Patient authorization: required elements, revocation rights
Minimum necessary standard for disclosures
Special protections: substance abuse (42 CFR Part 2), mental health, HIV
Electronic ROI systems and audit trails
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through three ROI scenarios: subpoena request, insurance audit, patient request - determining what can be released and what requires authorization

review.value: HIPAA principle requiring covered entities to limit PHI disclosures to the minimum amount needed to accomplish the purpose
Federal regulation providing extra privacy protections for substance use disorder treatment records beyond standard HIPAA

learning_objectives: Describe how health data supports analytics and reporting
Understand data warehousing concepts in healthcare
Evaluate data quality issues that impact analytical reliability
readings: AHIMA Data Analytics in Healthcare Toolkit

lecture_notes.introduction: Health data management creates the foundation for analytics. Without clean, well-governed data, analytics efforts produce unreliable results that can harm patients and organizations.
key_concepts: Healthcare data warehousing: ETL processes and dimensional modeling
Data quality impact on analytics: garbage in, garbage out
Registries and their role in clinical research and quality reporting
De-identification methods for research data sets (Safe Harbor, Expert Determination)
Business intelligence tools and dashboards in healthcare
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data quality assessment exercise: analyze a sample health dataset for completeness, accuracy, and consistency issues
setup_requirements: Sample dataset with intentional quality issues

review.value: Extract, Transform, Load - process of pulling data from source systems, cleaning/standardizing it, and loading into a data warehouse
HIPAA method requiring removal of 18 specific identifiers to create a de-identified dataset for research

questions.question: Explain the role of a data steward in a healthcare organization's data governance program. How does this role differ from a data owner?
questions.answer: A data steward is responsible for the day-to-day management and quality of data within a specific domain (e.g., clinical data, financial data). They define data quality rules, monitor data quality metrics, resolve data issues, and enforce data standards. A data owner is typically a senior leader who has accountability for a data domain and makes policy decisions about data access and use, but does not manage data quality operationally. The steward executes the governance policies the owner approves.

rubric.key: Accurate description of data steward responsibilities
Clear distinction from data owner role

questions.question: A patient is admitted with chest pain and diagnosed with acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction of the left anterior descending artery. During the stay, the patient develops acute kidney injury. Describe the coding principles that determine which diagnosis is sequenced as principal.
questions.answer: The principal diagnosis is defined as the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission. In this case, the acute STEMI of the LAD would be sequenced as the principal diagnosis because the chest pain (symptom) led to admission, and the STEMI was the definitive diagnosis established after study. The acute kidney injury would be coded as a secondary diagnosis because it developed during the stay, not before admission. The POA (Present on Admission) indicator would be Y for the STEMI and N for the AKI.

rubric.key: Proper handling of secondary diagnosis and POA indicators

questions.question: Compare centralized and federated health information exchange models. Under what circumstances might an organization prefer one over the other?
questions.answer: In a centralized HIE model, participating organizations send copies of patient data to a central repository where it is aggregated and made available to authorized users. In a federated model, data remains at the source organizations and is queried on demand through a record locator service. A centralized model might be preferred when fast access to comprehensive records is critical (e.g., emergency departments) and participants trust the central authority. A federated model is preferred when organizations want to maintain local control over their data, when data governance requirements vary, or when competitive concerns make centralized data aggregation unacceptable.

questions.question: A hospital receives a subpoena for a patient's complete medical record. The patient was treated for both a broken arm and substance use disorder during the same stay. What privacy considerations must the HIM department address before responding?
questions.answer: The HIM department must address multiple privacy layers: (1) HIPAA requires verification that the subpoena meets requirements - if it is not accompanied by a court order, the provider must ensure the patient was notified or a protective order was sought. (2) The substance use disorder records are subject to 42 CFR Part 2, which provides stricter protections than HIPAA. A standard subpoena is generally insufficient to release SUD records without a specific court order authorizing disclosure. (3) The department must apply the minimum necessary standard and may need to segregate the SUD-related portions from the general medical record. (4) The response must be documented in the disclosure audit log.

rubric.key: Identifies 42 CFR Part 2 additional protections for SUD records
Discusses minimum necessary and record segregation

major_assignments.title: Data Governance Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design a comprehensive data governance program for a mid-sized community hospital, including governance structure, data quality framework, policies, and implementation roadmap.
objectives: Apply data governance principles to a realistic healthcare scenario
Design a governance structure with clear roles and accountability
Develop data quality monitoring metrics and processes
deliverables: Data quality framework with KPIs and monitoring plan

criteria: Comprehensive governance structure appropriate for the scenario
Robust data quality framework with measurable KPIs

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of health data management concepts and regulations

hands_on_labs.description: Coding exercises, data quality assessments, and ROI scenario practice

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering governance, coding, and data lifecycle

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including HIE, privacy, and analytics foundations

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Lab sessions are mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day for up to 5 days. Extensions require 48 hours advance notice.
course_policies.collaboration: Coding exercises may be discussed but must be submitted individually. Group project components require documented equal contribution.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Proper citation required. Use of coding tools is permitted for exercises but answers must demonstrate understanding.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with internet access. ICD-10-CM browser, FHIR test server access, and spreadsheet software.
